title: Relative, absolute, fixed and sticky positioning

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling draft for Relative, absolute, fixed and sticky positioning; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CSS Snapshot 2024; W3C Working Group Note 2024; provisions applicable to Relative, absolute, fixed and sticky positioning
sources.claim: Positioned layout uses static, relative, absolute, fixed, or sticky positioning and a containing block that determines offsets.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Relative, absolute, fixed and sticky positioning
sources.claim: Positioned layout uses static, relative, absolute, fixed, or sticky positioning and a containing block that determines offsets.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Positioned layout uses static, relative, absolute, fixed, or sticky positioning and a containing block that determines offsets.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A badge is absolutely positioned inside a relatively positioned card whose padding reserves visual room. Your goal is to implement and debug a positioned component without covering content or focus targets. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Box model, sizing and overflow; html semantics landmarks; Normal flow and display; Overflow and containment. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Positioned layout uses static, relative, absolute, fixed, or sticky positioning and a containing block that determines offsets. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Transforms can create containing blocks, sticky needs a scroll context and inset, and out-of-flow elements do not reserve ordinary space. This draft therefore rejects: position:absolute always uses the viewport as its coordinate system.

includes: Choose positioned layout with correct containing blocks and document-flow consequences.
Implement and debug a positioned component without covering content or focus targets.
excludes: Transforms can create containing blocks, sticky needs a scroll context and inset, and out-of-flow elements do not reserve ordinary space.

outcomes: Implement and debug a positioned component without covering content or focus targets.

examples.title: Relative, absolute, fixed and sticky positioning: concrete case
examples.situation: A badge is absolutely positioned inside a relatively positioned card whose padding reserves visual room.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A badge is absolutely positioned inside a relatively positioned card whose padding reserves visual room.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Determine the containing block first; absolute offsets are not automatically relative to the viewport.
examples.result: The result follows because Determine the containing block first; absolute offsets are not automatically relative to the viewport.

examples.title: Relative, absolute, fixed and sticky positioning: boundary case
examples.situation: Transforms can create containing blocks, sticky needs a scroll context and inset, and out-of-flow elements do not reserve ordinary space.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Transforms can create containing blocks, sticky needs a scroll context and inset, and out-of-flow elements do not reserve ordinary space.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: position:absolute always uses the viewport as its coordinate system.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Determine the containing block first; absolute offsets are not automatically relative to the viewport.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: position:absolute always uses the viewport as its coordinate system.
misconceptions.correction: Determine the containing block first; absolute offsets are not automatically relative to the viewport.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling artifact to explain why this belief fails: position:absolute always uses the viewport as its coordinate system.

analogy.example: A layered stage design can model Relative, absolute, fixed and sticky positioning, where rules size, place, and paint existing content.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Relative, absolute, fixed and sticky positioning before using exact platform terms
limitations: Physical layers do not reproduce cascade algorithms, intrinsic sizing, writing modes, or user preferences.
